preFix.title: Dyeing with dahlia petals
preFix.subtitle: Warm yellow and orange tones from garden dahlias on alum-mordanted wool
preFix.excerpt: Dahlia petals give warm yellow to orange tones on alum-mordanted wool. Fresh or freeze-dried petals give the strongest colour; dried petals still produce a usable pale yellow. An iron modifier shifts the result toward olive-green.
preFix.sourceNotes:
- Dahlia (Dahlia spp.) as a natural dye source is documented in multiple public domain and contemporary natural dye references. No specific source quotation used.
- General mordanting procedure from standard public domain dye practice.

content.text: Dahlias (Dahlia spp.) are one of the most rewarding garden dye plants. The orange, red, and yellow varieties give the richest colour; pale cream dahlias give a weaker result. Fresh petals are best; freeze-dried petals work almost as well. Dried petals that have sat in storage for over a year may give only a faint yellow. This is a straightforward first
mordanting
and dyeing project, suitable for any beginner comfortable following a temperature sequence.
Dye pots and tools are dedicated and never used for food. Wear gloves and work in a ventilated space.

items: Dahlia petals: 200% weight of fibre (WOF) by dry weight (200 g petals per 100 g wool)
Pre-mordanted wool: mordanted with alum at 15% WOF
Iron mordant (ferrous sulphate) for optional modifier
Stainless steel dye pot, large enough that fibre moves freely
Muslin straining bag or old pillowcase for the petals

content.text: Method
Step 1: Pre-mordant the wool. Dissolve alum at 15%
weight of fibre

in warm water in the dye pot. Add the pre-wetted wool. Raise the temperature slowly to 80°C over 30-40 minutes. Hold at 80°C for 45 minutes, then allow to cool to 40°C before removing the wool. Squeeze gently; do not wring. The mordanted wool can be used immediately or dried for later use.

Step 2: Prepare the dye bath. Put the dahlia petals in the muslin straining bag (this makes removal easier and stops petal fragments sticking to the wool). Place the bag in the dye pot and cover with cold water. Bring to a gentle simmer (approximately 80-90°C) and hold for 30 minutes. Remove and discard the petal bag.
Step 3: Allow the dye bath to cool to approximately 50°C. Add the pre-wetted mordanted wool to the dye bath. Raise the temperature slowly to 80°C over 20 minutes.
Step 4: Hold at 80°C for 45 minutes, turning the wool gently every 10 minutes. Do not allow the bath to boil; above 90°C wool can felt.
Step 5: Allow the bath to cool to below 40°C. Remove the wool, squeeze gently, and rinse in water of the same temperature as the dye bath to avoid thermal shock. Rinse until the water runs clear.
Step 6: Hang to dry away from direct sunlight.
Mordant variations
The
mordant
used significantly changes the resulting colour:
Alum (standard): warm yellow to golden orange, depending on dahlia variety and petal freshness.
Iron modifier: add ferrous sulphate at 2%
weight of fibre
dissolved in a small cup of hot water, stirred into the dye bath in the last 10 minutes of dyeing. Shifts yellow to olive-green or khaki. Use the
iron modifier
sparingly: too much makes the wool feel harsh and can damage the fibre over time.
No mordant (unmordanted wool): a very pale yellow that will fade quickly.
Light-fastness
is poor without a mordant.
Wastewater disposal
An alum dye bath is mildly acidic. Allow it to cool fully. The spent
exhaust bath

can go down a household drain in small amounts. If you used an iron modifier, pour that spent bath onto garden soil rather than down the drain. At home-dyeing concentrations, iron sulphate is harmless and benefits acid-loving plants.

items.problem: The colour is very pale even with fresh petals
items.solution: The petal-to-fibre ratio may be too low, or the dye bath was not simmered long enough to fully extract the pigment. Try increasing the petal quantity to 300% WOF, or simmer the petal bath for 45 minutes before straining.

items.problem: The colour is uneven with patches or streaks
items.solution: The wool was not adequately pre-wetted before entering the dye bath, or the temperature was raised too quickly. Pre-wet the wool in lukewarm water for at least 30 minutes, and raise the dye bath temperature no faster than 5°C per 5 minutes.

items.problem: The colour fades significantly on the first wash
items.solution: Check that the alum mordanting step was completed correctly, including the full 45-minute hold time at 80°C. Insufficient mordanting is the most common cause of poor colour retention in natural dyeing.

initialFindings.message: paragraph reads at grade 11.8 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
initialFindings.snippet: Alum (standard): warm yellow to golden orange, depending on dahlia variety and petal freshness.
